APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper is well within the scope of a top-tier NLP/ML venue: dialogue state tracking is a core NLP problem and the work evaluates on standard benchmarks (DSTC2, WOZ 2.0) against strong baselines (Henderson et al., Wen et al.) using community metrics (joint goal accuracy, requests). The methodology and evaluation meet venue expectations (pretrained vectors, two neural variants, context modelling, hyperparameters reported, and thorough related work coverage), and the audience of researchers working on dialogue and representation learning is appropriate. Minor reproducibility concern (code not mentioned) does not affect topical fit. | B(5): This paper is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues: it addresses dialogue state tracking, a core task in conversational AI, and proposes a novel, data-driven Neural Belief Tracker that removes reliance on hand-crafted lexicons. The methodology uses established neural techniques (pre-trained word vectors, DNN/CNN variants, context gating) and evaluates on standard benchmarks (DSTC2, WOZ 2.0) with accepted metrics (joint goal accuracy, requests), comparing to strong baselines. Its emphasis on scalability and reduced annotation/lexicon dependence aligns well with the venue’s audience and priorities in dialogue research. Overall the paper is well positioned for a top-tier conference.

The methodology and evaluation meet venue expectations (pretrained vectors, two neural variants, context modelling, hyperparameters reported, and thorough related work coverage), and the audience of researchers working on dialogue and representation learning is appropriate. Minor reproducibility concern (code not mentioned) does not affect topical fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues: it addresses dialogue state tracking, a core task in conversational AI, and proposes a novel, data-driven Neural Belief Tracker that removes reliance on hand-crafted lexicons. The methodology uses established neural techniques (pre-trained word vectors, DNN/CNN variants, context gating) and evaluates on standard benchmarks (DSTC2, WOZ 2.0) with accepted metrics (joint goal accuracy, requests), comparing to strong baselines. Its emphasis on scalability and reduced annotation/lexicon dependence aligns well with the venue’s audience and priorities in dialogue research. Overall the paper is well positioned for a top-tier conference.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable and focused novelty over prior DST work by introducing the Neural Belief Tracker architecture that composes fixed pre-trained word vectors into utterance/context representations and, importantly, a context-gating mechanism that conditions on the last system acts to make per slot-value binary decisions. These elements materially differentiate it from earlier deep-learning and rule-based DST efforts (e.g. 2013 neural/CRF-like trackers and probabilistic rule systems) and from delexicalisation approaches that rely on hand-crafted lexica. While the components (pre-trained vectors, CNN/DNN encoders, and gating) are individually familiar, their specific combination and the Markovian context modelling tailored to belief tracking represent a non-trivial and useful extension that would be of interest to experts in the area. | B(4): The paper presents a notable and well-differentiated contribution: a neural, data-driven belief tracker that leverages pre-trained word vectors to avoid hand-crafted semantic lexicons and introduces context gating mechanisms to incorporate recent system acts—features not present in earlier DSTC-era neural or rule-based trackers (e.g. 2013 DNN and probabilistic rule approaches). The two NBT variants and explicit modelling of request/confirm acts represent a non-trivial architectural advance over delexicalisation-based systems and prior neural attempts, and the authors clearly separate their method from lexicon-dependent baselines. While the ideas build on established components (word embeddings, CNN/DNN encoders), their combination and application to scalable, lexicon-free belief tracking is a meaningful and informative step for experts in dialogue state tracking.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable and focused novelty over prior DST work by introducing the Neural Belief Tracker architecture that composes fixed pre-trained word vectors into utterance/context representations and, importantly, a context-gating mechanism that conditions on the last system acts to make per slot-value binary decisions. These elements materially differentiate it from earlier deep-learning and rule-based DST efforts (e.g. 2013 neural/CRF-like trackers and probabilistic rule systems) and from delexicalisation approaches that rely on hand-crafted lexica. While the components (pre-trained vectors, CNN/DNN encoders, and gating) are individually familiar, their specific combination and the Markovian context modelling tailored to belief tracking represent a non-trivial and useful extension that would be of interest to experts in the area.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable and well-differentiated contribution: a neural, data-driven belief tracker that leverages pre-trained word vectors to avoid hand-crafted semantic lexicons and introduces context gating mechanisms to incorporate recent system acts—features not present in earlier DSTC-era neural or rule-based trackers (e.g. 2013 DNN and probabilistic rule approaches). The two NBT variants and explicit modelling of request/confirm acts represent a non-trivial architectural advance over delexicalisation-based systems and prior neural attempts, and the authors clearly separate their method from lexicon-dependent baselines. While the ideas build on established components (word embeddings, CNN/DNN encoders), their combination and application to scalable, lexicon-free belief tracking is a meaningful and informative step for experts in dialogue state tracking.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper tackles an important practical bottleneck in dialogue state tracking—reliance on hand-crafted semantic lexicons and SLU annotations—by proposing a data-driven Neural Belief Tracker that matches state-of-the-art while removing expensive lexicons (and outperforming baselines when lexicons are absent). From my reproducibility-focused perspective, demonstrating strong results on DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0 and introducing context gating and two architecture variants makes the approach immediately relevant to practitioners seeking scalable DST solutions. The reliance on fixed pre-trained embeddings (and discussion of their limitations) and the Markovian context assumption leave clear follow-up avenues (embedding specialization, richer context modelling), so the work is likely to be built upon broadly even if not wholly transformative. | B(4): This work tackles an important practical bottleneck in dialogue state tracking—reliance on hand-crafted semantic lexicons and expensive SLU annotations—by introducing the Neural Belief Tracker that composes pre-trained word vectors and context gating to match or outperform lexicon-based SOTA on DSTC2/WOZ 2.0. By demonstrating competitive performance without task-specific dictionaries and proposing context modelling and two neural variants (NBT-DNN, NBT-CNN), it is likely to change practitioner thinking about scalable, data-driven DST and spur follow-up work on distributional semantic integration and context mechanisms. Limitations (sensitivity to vector antonymy and only modelling the last system act) constrain immediate deployment in all settings but do not diminish the paper’s strong potential to open new research directions and wider adoption.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper tackles an important practical bottleneck in dialogue state tracking—reliance on hand-crafted semantic lexicons and SLU annotations—by proposing a data-driven Neural Belief Tracker that matches state-of-the-art while removing expensive lexicons (and outperforming baselines when lexicons are absent). From my reproducibility-focused perspective, demonstrating strong results on DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0 and introducing context gating and two architecture variants makes the approach immediately relevant to practitioners seeking scalable DST solutions. The reliance on fixed pre-trained embeddings (and discussion of their limitations) and the Markovian context assumption leave clear follow-up avenues (embedding specialization, richer context modelling), so the work is likely to be built upon broadly even if not wholly transformative.

reviewer_b.justification: This work tackles an important practical bottleneck in dialogue state tracking—reliance on hand-crafted semantic lexicons and expensive SLU annotations—by introducing the Neural Belief Tracker that composes pre-trained word vectors and context gating to match or outperform lexicon-based SOTA on DSTC2/WOZ 2.0. By demonstrating competitive performance without task-specific dictionaries and proposing context modelling and two neural variants (NBT-DNN, NBT-CNN), it is likely to change practitioner thinking about scalable, data-driven DST and spur follow-up work on distributional semantic integration and context mechanisms. Limitations (sensitivity to vector antonymy and only modelling the last system act) constrain immediate deployment in all settings but do not diminish the paper’s strong potential to open new research directions and wider adoption.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(5): The paper evaluates on standard DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0 benchmarks and reports joint-goal and request accuracies, directly comparing to strong baselines (Henderson et al. delexicalisation model and Wen et al. 2017) including variants augmented with hand-crafted semantic dictionaries. Comparisons appear fair (same datasets and metrics) and the authors explicitly acknowledge when prior models match or exceed performance depending on lexicon use, while analysing how NBT design choices (context gating, DNN vs CNN variants, pretrained vector choices) drive differences. Related prior work in dialogue state tracking is discussed thoroughly, so experimental comparisons are comprehensive and reproducible in scope. | B(4): The paper compares against strong, relevant baselines (Henderson et al. delexicalisation model and Wen et al.) on the standard DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0 datasets using joint-goal and request accuracy, and explicitly evaluates conditions with and without task-specific semantic dictionaries. Comparisons appear fair and measured (same data and metrics), and the authors acknowledge when prior methods match or exceed performance (e.g. on WOZ 2.0) and analyze where pretrained vectors help or hurt. Coverage of related work is described as thorough, though a couple of earlier neural and rule-based DST approaches (e.g. early DNN/CRF and probabilistic rule-based trackers) could be discussed more empirically—hence a minor omission rather than a critical gap.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates on standard DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0 benchmarks and reports joint-goal and request accuracies, directly comparing to strong baselines (Henderson et al. delexicalisation model and Wen et al. 2017) including variants augmented with hand-crafted semantic dictionaries. Comparisons appear fair (same datasets and metrics) and the authors explicitly acknowledge when prior models match or exceed performance depending on lexicon use, while analysing how NBT design choices (context gating, DNN vs CNN variants, pretrained vector choices) drive differences. Related prior work in dialogue state tracking is discussed thoroughly, so experimental comparisons are comprehensive and reproducible in scope.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against strong, relevant baselines (Henderson et al. delexicalisation model and Wen et al.) on the standard DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0 datasets using joint-goal and request accuracy, and explicitly evaluates conditions with and without task-specific semantic dictionaries. Comparisons appear fair and measured (same data and metrics), and the authors acknowledge when prior methods match or exceed performance (e.g. on WOZ 2.0) and analyze where pretrained vectors help or hurt. Coverage of related work is described as thorough, though a couple of earlier neural and rule-based DST approaches (e.g. early DNN/CRF and probabilistic rule-based trackers) could be discussed more empirically—hence a minor omission rather than a critical gap.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear flow from introduction through methods and experiments, and the main contributions (NBT framework, context modelling, NBT variants) are stated early. Notation and hyperparameters are reported, and related work coverage is thorough, which supports reproducibility. Figures/tables and examples seem effective enough to convey results (DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0 comparisons, joint goal/request metrics). Minor presentation issues remain (e.g., clearer discussion of the Markovian context modelling limitation and the impact of distributional vectors on antonyms), so I do not rate it a perfect 5. | B(4): The paper is generally well organized and follows a clear progression from motivation through method, experiments, and conclusions; the main contributions are stated early and contextualised against delexicalisation baselines. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, while figures/tables and dataset comparisons (DSTC2, WOZ 2.0) effectively support the claims. Writing quality is good with limited ambiguity, though a few sections (e.g. the context-gating/Markovian assumption and its practical implications) could be expanded for readers less familiar with dialogue-act modelling. Overall the presentation is strong with only minor clarifications needed.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears well-organized with a clear flow from introduction through methods and experiments, and the main contributions (NBT framework, context modelling, NBT variants) are stated early. Notation and hyperparameters are reported, and related work coverage is thorough, which supports reproducibility. Figures/tables and examples seem effective enough to convey results (DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0 comparisons, joint goal/request metrics). Minor presentation issues remain (e.g., clearer discussion of the Markovian context modelling limitation and the impact of distributional vectors on antonyms), so I do not rate it a perfect 5.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is generally well organized and follows a clear progression from motivation through method, experiments, and conclusions; the main contributions are stated early and contextualised against delexicalisation baselines. Overall the presentation is strong with only minor clarifications needed.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper clearly states its main claims and presents empirical evidence on standard DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0 benchmarks, comparing against relevant baselines (including delexicalisation and dictionary-augmented systems) and reporting joint-goal and request accuracies with hyperparameters disclosed. However, from the summary there is no mention of multiple random seeds, error bars, or statistical tests, and code is not reported released, which weakens reproducibility and confidence in small improvements. There are also acknowledged modelling limitations (fixed pre-trained vectors, Markovian one-step context) that are not fully ablated. Overall the experiments largely support the claims, but missing robustness checks and release of code/hyperparameter search details prevent a flawless technical evaluation. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and backed by empirical results on DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0 showing NBT matches or exceeds baselines that require hand-crafted lexicons, and the design (fixed pre-trained vectors, NBT-DNN/CNN, context gating) is described and evaluated. There are no formal mathematical proofs to check, and the architectures and hyperparameters are reported. However, the methodology omits mention of statistical significance testing and the context model is Markovian (only last system acts), which are acknowledged limitations that slightly weaken claims about general robustness to dialogue context and real-world noise. Overall the technical evidence supports the claims but with these minor methodological gaps.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and presents empirical evidence on standard DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0 benchmarks, comparing against relevant baselines (including delexicalisation and dictionary-augmented systems) and reporting joint-goal and request accuracies with hyperparameters disclosed. However, from the summary there is no mention of multiple random seeds, error bars, or statistical tests, and code is not reported released, which weakens reproducibility and confidence in small improvements. There are also acknowledged modelling limitations (fixed pre-trained vectors, Markovian one-step context) that are not fully ablated. Overall the experiments largely support the claims, but missing robustness checks and release of code/hyperparameter search details prevent a flawless technical evaluation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and backed by empirical results on DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0 showing NBT matches or exceeds baselines that require hand-crafted lexicons, and the design (fixed pre-trained vectors, NBT-DNN/CNN, context gating) is described and evaluated. There are no formal mathematical proofs to check, and the architectures and hyperparameters are reported. However, the methodology omits mention of statistical significance testing and the context model is Markovian (only last system acts), which are acknowledged limitations that slightly weaken claims about general robustness to dialogue context and real-world noise. Overall the technical evidence supports the claims but with these minor methodological gaps.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are solid: evaluation on DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0 and comparisons to strong delexicalisation baselines (including dictionary-augmented variants) support the main claims, and two NBT variants plus investigations of pre-trained vector effects are reported. The paper includes some sensitivity analysis (different vector specialisation) and discusses concrete limitations (antonym issues, Markovian context), but lacks full robustness checks (e.g. multiple random seeds, statistical significance) and broader ablations isolating each model component’s contribution. Related work appears thorough and hyperparameters are reported, though code is not mentioned which modestly reduces reproducibility. Overall the empirical work is careful and convincing but misses a few reproducibility and robustness safeguards I would expect from a reproducible-methodology perspective. | B(4): The paper evaluates NBT on two standard datasets (DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0) and compares to strong baselines including the delexicalisation tracker (Henderson et al.) and Wen et al., showing performance both with and without semantic lexicons, which supports its main claims. Related work coverage is reported as thorough, and the authors discuss limitations (e.g. issues with distributional vectors and the Markovian context assumption) and report hyperparameters, aiding reproducibility. However, the summary does not indicate thorough ablation or sensitivity analyses beyond the two NBT variants and context gating, nor is code availability mentioned, leaving some gaps for full reproducibility and deeper analysis of failure modes. Overall the experimental evaluation is solid but not exhaustively analyzed, so a 4 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are solid: evaluation on DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0 and comparisons to strong delexicalisation baselines (including dictionary-augmented variants) support the main claims, and two NBT variants plus investigations of pre-trained vector effects are reported. The paper includes some sensitivity analysis (different vector specialisation) and discusses concrete limitations (antonym issues, Markovian context), but lacks full robustness checks (e.g. multiple random seeds, statistical significance) and broader ablations isolating each model component’s contribution. Related work appears thorough and hyperparameters are reported, though code is not mentioned which modestly reduces reproducibility. Overall the empirical work is careful and convincing but misses a few reproducibility and robustness safeguards I would expect from a reproducible-methodology perspective.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates NBT on two standard datasets (DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0) and compares to strong baselines including the delexicalisation tracker (Henderson et al.) and Wen et al., showing performance both with and without semantic lexicons, which supports its main claims. Related work coverage is reported as thorough, and the authors discuss limitations (e.g. issues with distributional vectors and the Markovian context assumption) and report hyperparameters, aiding reproducibility. However, the summary does not indicate thorough ablation or sensitivity analyses beyond the two NBT variants and context gating, nor is code availability mentioned, leaving some gaps for full reproducibility and deeper analysis of failure modes. Overall the experimental evaluation is solid but not exhaustively analyzed, so a 4 is appropriate.

main_claims: Proposes the Neural Belief Tracker (NBT), a data-driven framework for dialogue state tracking that reasons over pre-trained word vectors and composes them into distributed representations of user utterances and dialogue context.
NBT overcomes reliance on hand-crafted semantic lexicons or expensive SLU annotations, matching state-of-the-art models that use such lexicons and outperforming them when lexicons are not provided.
Introduces context modelling (including handling of system request and confirm acts) and two NBT variants (NBT-DNN and NBT-CNN) for improved belief tracking.
_summary.methodology: The NBT framework uses pre-trained word vectors (kept fixed) and neural architectures (NBT-DNN and NBT-CNN) to compose utterance and context representations and make binary decisions per slot-value pair; context gating mechanisms incorporate the last system acts to influence predictions.
baselines: Delexicalisation-based model of Henderson et al. (2014c/2014d)
Belief tracking model of Wen et al. (2017)
Baseline supplemented with task-specific semantic dictionary (provided by baseline creators)
limitations: Performance gains from semantically specialised pre-trained vectors are weaker on the clean WOZ 2.0 dataset compared to noisy DSTC2.
Distributional word vectors (e.g. GloVe) can keep related but antonymous words close together, which can offset useful semantic content and hurt performance.
Delexicalisation-based baseline methods require hand-crafted semantic dictionaries, hindering scaling to larger domains.
Context modelling in NBT makes a Markovian decision to only consider the last system acts (i.e. only the most recent system act is modelled).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - dialogue state tracking (spoken dialogue systems)

reviewer_a.name: Reproducible Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with a decade of experience building and evaluating dialogue and NLP systems in both academic and industrial settings; extensive experience with experimental design, ablation studies, and reproducible research practices.
traits: Insists on clear experimental setup: datasets, splits, training/validation/test procedures, and hyperparameter search protocols
Values strong baselines and ablation studies that isolate contributions of model components
Looks for statistical significance, error bars, and robustness checks (e.g., multiple runs, sensitivity to random seeds)
Seeks implementation details necessary for reproducibility: pre-processing, initialization, training time, and whether pretrained components are frozen or fine-tuned
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on whether the experiments and reporting convincingly support the claims, and whether the work can be reliably reproduced and compared to alternatives.

reviewer_b.name: Dialogue Systems Impact Scholar
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher in spoken dialogue systems and conversational AI with a track record of work on dialogue state tracking, evaluation metrics, and deploying dialogue models in real-world settings.
traits: Evaluates novelty relative to prior state-tracking and semantic representation approaches and how the work advances the field
Assesses practical impact: scalability, ability to handle real user language variation, and dependence on external resources (e.g., lexicons, annotated SLU)
Looks for meaningful qualitative analysis: failure modes, linguistic/generalization behavior, and how model handles context and dialogue acts
Values connections to downstream applications and clarity about limitations and deployment feasibility
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Judges the paper on significance and fit within the dialogue research landscape, emphasizing novelty, real-world applicability, and insightful qualitative analysis.
